claim: connectAgent (src/remote-client/bootstrap.ts:126-270) and connectDaemonAgent (src/remote-client/bootstrap-daemon.ts:45-210) duplicate ~140 lines of near-identical SSH-exec-channel spawn/decode/handshake-pump/teardown logic (child spawn, abort wiring, stderr tail tracking, decoder pump, handshake establishment, session handle construction, exit handling) instead of factoring the shared pump into one parameterized helper (varying only the launch-command builder and the outcome classifier). The duplication has already drifted: bootstrap.ts's stderr line filter is 'trimmed.length > 0', while bootstrap-daemon.ts additionally excludes bridge-status lines and separately accumulates stderrAll for bridge-status parsing — a future bugfix to the shared pump (e.g. an abort race, a decoder-dead edge case) has to be remembered and applied twice.
fix: Extract the common exec/decode/handshake pump into one shared function in bootstrap.ts (or a new bootstrap-core.ts) parameterized by: the argv/launch-command builder, an outcome classifier fn, and an optional stderr-accumulator hook for bridge-status parsing. Have both connectAgent and connectDaemonAgent call the shared pump so protocol-pump fixes land once.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-06 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/cleanup, out of scope for this fix pass, tracked for a future hardening feature.

claim: isAlive(pid) is defined identically in both src/agent/bridge.ts:33-40 and src/agent/daemon-server.ts:245-252, and readMetadata (bridge.ts:42-51) / readMetadataSafe (daemon-server.ts:254-263) are structurally identical (existsSync -> readFileSync -> JSON.parse -> validateDaemonMetadata -> null-on-throw), just under different local names/return-type shapes. src/agent/daemon-guard.ts already exists specifically as 'the single-instance / stale-reclaim decision core... shared by daemon-server.ts (writer) and bridge.ts (reader)' and already houses validateDaemonMetadata/decideDaemonReach, so this is the designated place for these two helpers but they were left duplicated instead of consolidated there.
fix: Move isAlive and a metadata-read-with-validate helper (parameterized on an injected fs seam if needed for unit-testability) into daemon-guard.ts, and have both bridge.ts and daemon-server.ts import them from there.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-06 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/cleanup, out of scope for this fix pass, tracked for a future hardening feature.

claim: src/agent/daemon-server.ts's claimSocket (lines 315-340) reimplements a bounded poll-connect loop independently of bridge.ts's pollConnect/tryConnectOnce (lines 88-98/63-84), using five unrelated, undocumented magic numbers (maxAttempts=200, an inner probe loop of 15, and delays of 100ms/25ms/50ms) instead of named constants. The resulting worst-case wall-clock bound for claimSocket (up to roughly 200 * (15 * 125ms + 50ms) ~= several minutes) is undocumented and not reconciled with DAEMON_SPAWN_WAIT_MS (10s), which is the only bound named in the spec/constants file for readiness waits — a reader has no way to tell whether this is intentional or an oversight.
fix: Extract one shared bounded poll-connect helper (parameterized by per-attempt timeout and overall deadline) used by both claimSocket and bridge.ts's pollConnect, and either derive claimSocket's retry budget from a named exported constant (documented against DAEMON_SPAWN_WAIT_MS) or explain in a comment why the daemon's own bind-claim loop intentionally uses a much longer bound than the bridge's readiness wait.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-06 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/cleanup, out of scope for this fix pass, tracked for a future hardening feature.

claim: DaemonLifecycle.dispose() (src/agent/daemon-lifecycle.ts:104-108, part of the exported DaemonLifecycle interface) is never called anywhere in production code — runDaemon in daemon-server.ts wires onBind/onUnbind/onPaneSpawned/onPaneExited but never calls lifecycle.dispose(), and the only caller of .dispose() in the whole repo is tests/agent-daemon-lifecycle.test.ts. It is effectively dead public API surface, and its absence from runDaemon's cleanup() means there is no code path that idempotently tears down the lifecycle's timer/signal-handler state outside of the lifecycle's own normal shutdown firing.
fix: Either call lifecycle.dispose() from runDaemon's cleanup() (defensive, idempotent, and documents the intended teardown order) or remove dispose() from the public DaemonLifecycle interface if it is truly test-only scaffolding.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-06 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/cleanup, out of scope for this fix pass, tracked for a future hardening feature.

claim: REQ-003 requires the daemon socket be owner-only 0600 'enforced before or atomically with accepting the first connection'. The real implementation instead binds first, then chmods: daemon-server.ts's listenOnce()/claimSocket() calls net.createServer().listen(socketPath) and only later (after the async claim resolves) calls chmodSync(socketPath, 0o600) (lines ~385-391); the pure bootstrapDaemonEndpoint seam mirrors the identical listen-then-chmod order (lines 79-82). Once listen() succeeds at the OS level, the kernel starts accepting connections immediately at the socket's default creation-time mode (0666/0777 minus the process's umask -- e.g. 0644/0755 under a common umask 022, or fully open under umask 000), so on many real deployments any other local user on the shared remote host can connect during the window before chmodSync executes -- reaching the full F15/F16/F17/F18/F20 protocol surface (attach to live PTYs, steal the F20 lease, read/write another user's remote session) before the daemon is actually secured. The spec's own acceptance wording ('chmod-before-listen-callback') conflates the JS listen callback with kernel-level accept-enablement, which is the root of the gap: it lets a non-atomic chmod-after-listen implementation appear compliant. No test exercises this against a real socket/filesystem -- the only ordering test (tests/agent-daemon-guard.test.ts TEST-2411) drives fully synchronous injected seams that cannot model the real kernel accept race, so CI cannot catch it.
fix: Wrap the real bind in a temporarily restrictive process.umask(0o077) (save/restore the prior umask immediately before/after the listen() call) so the socket file is created 0600 from the instant it exists, eliminating the window rather than narrowing it. Apply the same fix in both daemon-server.ts's real listenOnce()/claimSocket() path and (for parity) document that the pure bootstrapDaemonEndpoint seam models this via its own listen seam. Add a real (non-seamed) POSIX integration test that races a raw net.connect() attempt started concurrently with the real bind/listen and asserts the connection is refused (permission denied) if it lands before chmod would have run under the old ordering -- i.e. prove no window exists, not just that the calls happen in the right order.
resolution: Verified fixed by direct code read: daemon-server.ts's bootstrapDaemonEndpoint (pure seam, lines 74-101) and listenOnce (real path, lines 341-358) both now wrap the bind in process.umask(0o077) saved immediately before and restored immediately after listen() settles (success or error), so the socket is created 0600 from the instant it exists -- no chmod-after-listen window. Buffer.byteLength is used for the AF_UNIX path-length guard (FINDING-007's fix, same file). A new real (non-seamed) POSIX integration test, TEST-2454 in tests/agent-daemon-process.test.ts (describe.skipIf(win32)), spawns the real daemon process and hot-polls statSync on the real socket path, asserting mode===0o600 at first observable existence -- exactly the 'prove no window exists' remediation requested. Test is skip-gated on this Windows dev box but the code path and test logic are correct on inspection; ran the adjacent seam-based suites (agent-daemon-guard/core/lifecycle) green (26/26).

claim: Stale-socket RECLAIM removes agent.sock/daemon.json OUTSIDE any serialized critical section. In claimSocket the sequence [last probe returns not-connectable] -> readMetadataSafe -> decideDaemonReach -> safeUnlink(socketPath)/safeUnlink(metadataPath) is unguarded across processes: the probe loop is a heuristic mitigation, not a mutex. Under adversarial interleaving a concurrent winner W that binds in the window (or between W's listen() and W's later writeFileSync(metadata) where daemon.json is briefly ABSENT) is seen by the loser as 'refused + absent metadata' -> reclaim -> unlink of W's LIVE socket, after which the loser rebinds the freed path => TWO listeners bound to the same agent dir (split-brain), and later W.cleanup()/idle-exit unconditionally unlinks the path it may no longer own, orphaning a daemon (the exact orphan-accumulation locked decision D3 rejects). This violates REQ-005 'at most ONE daemon' under concurrency and CONV-045 (a client-side pre-write check with no server compare-and-set MUST be inside the write's serialized critical section OR the residual race MUST be disclosed - neither holds; the spec asserts race-proofness). REQ-007's acceptance requires the reclaim-vs-concurrent interleaving to 'ride the REQ-005 vector', but the only interleaved real-process vector TEST-2424 starts two daemons on a FRESH dir (no remnants -> reclaim branch never taken) and TEST-2425 exercises reclaim only SEQUENTIALLY (single bridge, pre-seeded remnants) - so no test enforces the 'provably impossible by the REQ-005 guarantee' clause the spec relies on (CONV-060).
fix: Serialize reclaim+rebind behind a real critical section keyed on the agent dir - the plan's Q3-anticipated O_EXCL lockfile whose holder alone may unlink stale remnants and then bind, so a stale-remnant unlink can NEVER remove a concurrent winner's live socket; and make cleanup()/idle-exit only unlink files this process still owns (re-check ownership, e.g. metadata pid == process.pid, before removal). If a residual race is instead accepted, disclose it explicitly per CONV-045. Add an interleaved reclaim-under-concurrency vector per REQ-007+CONV-060: pre-seed dead-pid remnants AND start two real --daemon/--attach processes with overlapping bind windows, asserting exactly one listener and no live socket ever unlinked. propose CONV: a filesystem-remnant reclaim (unlink-then-recreate of a socket/lock/marker file) guarding a single-instance guarantee MUST perform the unlink inside the same serialized critical section that performs the recreate, and MUST only remove a remnant it has proven it owns - never on the strength of a prior liveness probe alone.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-06 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/cleanup, out of scope for this fix pass, tracked for a future hardening feature.

claim: The over-long AF_UNIX guard compares socketPath.length (UTF-16 code-unit count) to AF_UNIX_PATH_MAX=107, but the sun_path ceiling is 107 BYTES, not characters. A non-ASCII socket path - reachable in production because the default is <agentDir>/agent.sock under a home dir that may contain non-ASCII (e.g. /home/<multibyte-username>/.termhalla/agent/agent.sock, where each 3-byte UTF-8 char counts as 1 UTF-16 unit) - can be <=107 chars yet >107 bytes, passing the guard and then failing net.Server.listen() with a raw EINVAL. That is exactly the outcome REQ-003 forbids ('MUST fail daemon start with a specific error naming the path and the limit, never a raw EINVAL'). The behavior is locale/encoding-dependent: identical logical path, different result depending on byte encoding. TEST-2412 only exercises an all-ASCII 'x'.repeat(200) path, so the byte-vs-char discrepancy is never covered.
fix: Compare Buffer.byteLength(socketPath, 'utf8') (not socketPath.length) against AF_UNIX_PATH_MAX so the named-error guard is byte-accurate and the raw EINVAL is unreachable per REQ-003. Add a multibyte-path vector to TEST-2412 (a path <=107 chars but >107 bytes) asserting the named error, not an EINVAL.
resolution: Verified in src/agent/daemon-server.ts:79 — bootstrapDaemonEndpoint now compares Buffer.byteLength(socketPath, utf8) (not socketPath.length) against AF_UNIX_PATH_MAX=107 and returns a named error citing the encoded byte count and the limit before any listen seam call; TEST-2412 adds the multibyte vector (<=107 UTF-16 units, >107 bytes) asserting the named error, never EINVAL. The specific byte-vs-char comparison defect is fixed. Note: filed a separate determinism finding that this byte-correct guard lives only in the test-only seam and is not wired into the production runDaemon/claimSocket/listenOnce bind path.

claim: The idle-lifecycle tracks bound connections as a BOOLEAN (daemon-lifecycle.ts bound flag), but daemon-server wires onBind/onUnbind per established connection over a shared daemon that serves MANY concurrent connections (REQ-002). With two-or-more established connections and zero live panes, one connection ending flips bound to false while another connection is still established, so maybeArm arms the idle timer and the daemon self-exits, dropping a live bound connection. This violates REQ-006 MUST (self-exit only when zero panes AND no bound connection continuously). Reachable via two Termhalla windows/workspaces to the same host+user sharing the ~/.termhalla/agent daemon, or a reconnect overlap. The composition is UNTESTED: REQ-006 acceptance exercises only single-connection transitions and REQ-002 multi-connection scenarios never assert idle-timer state.
fix: Track an established-connection COUNT in the lifecycle (increment per establish, decrement per non-revoked end) and arm only when count===0 AND paneCount===0; add a test with two concurrent established connections where one ends and assert the daemon stays alive.
resolution: Verified fixed in src/agent/daemon-lifecycle.ts:53-116: the boolean bound is replaced by establishedCount; maybeArm() arms only when establishedCount===0 AND paneCount===0; onConnectionEstablished increments+cancels, onConnectionEnded decrements+maybeArm. Wired in daemon-server.ts:516-537 from the front-handshake observer (onConnectionEstablished at handshake completion, daemon-server.ts:273) and the session shutdown callback (onConnectionEnded on every established-connection end path incl. lease displacement, daemon-server.ts:245-247). Tested in agent-daemon-lifecycle.test.ts:116-147: two concurrent established connections, one ends -> timer MUST NOT arm; second ends -> arms; a lease-steal displacement never reaches count 0.

location: REQ-008 / src/agent/daemon-server.ts (one shared store+lease per agent dir)
claim: Consolidating to ONE daemon (one AgentSessionStore, one F20 exclusive single-holder lease) per host+user agent dir (DEFAULT_REMOTE_AGENT_DIR ~/.termhalla/agent, fixed) serializes ALL concurrent attachments to that host. Pre-0024 each ssh connection spawned its OWN agent process and store, so two remote workspaces/windows to the same host were independent. Post-0024 they share one store and one lease, so a second workspace binding STEALS the lease from the first (lease:revoked, disconnected banner) and they ping-pong. This is a material user-visible behavior change for the supported multi-window/multi-workspace-to-same-host workflow, undocumented despite the spec applying CONV-054 consequence-stating discipline to the version-drift lockout (REQ-012) but not to this one.
fix: Decide and document the intended behavior: either state the tmux attach-d single-attach-per-host consequence in remote-agent.md/remote-workspaces.md per CONV-054 (as REQ-012 did for drift), or scope the daemon/lease per-workspace rather than per-host if simultaneous same-host attachments must be preserved.
resolution: Verified fixed by the per-workspace daemon design (REQ-018): sockets/metadata/log are workspace-keyed (daemon-constants.ts:27-34 socketFileName/metadataFileName/logFileName(wsToken)); ws-token.ts deriveWsToken yields distinct tokens per distinct workspace id; production wiring passes daemon:{workspaceId} (services.ts:109) threaded from entry.workspaceId (remote-workspace-manager.ts:325). Two same-host workspaces now resolve independent sockets/daemons/leases — no shared AgentSessionStore, no cross-workspace lease steal — so the pre-0024 same-host coexistence is preserved by construction rather than merely documented as a single-attach consequence.

claim: daemon.log is only truncated at daemon START; there is no size cap or rotation WITHIN a generation. Because this feature makes the agent long-lived (a daemon with continuous sessions never idles out and can run for days), the spec CONV-003 claim of bounded growth per generation is illusory. A peer streaming skippable malformed frames hits the session.ts frame-skipped/session-continues path (session.ts:259/:280), which appends a diagnostic per frame WITHOUT ending the connection, an owner-local unbounded disk-growth vector on the remote. Pre-daemonization the log was bounded by the connection lifetime.
fix: Add a byte-size cap with rotation (or ring-buffer truncation) to appendLog so a long-lived generation cannot grow the log unbounded; assert the cap by test to make CONV-003 real. propose CONV: a process designed to run indefinitely (daemon/service) MUST bound its own diagnostics log by size, not only per process generation.
resolution: Verified fixed in src/agent/daemon-server.ts:115-142: createDaemonLogSink ring-truncates its buffer so the file never exceeds DAEMON_LOG_MAX_BYTES (1 MiB, daemon-constants.ts:22) WITHIN a generation, in addition to truncate-at-start. Tested TEST-2445 (agent-daemon-contract.test.ts:155-181): drives >4x the cap through the sink and asserts statSync(size) <= cap while the latest diagnostic survives. NOTE: separately filed FINDING-020 (MEDIUM) on the full-buffer-rewrite-per-append write amplification of this specific implementation.

claim: The detached daemon is spawned with stdio [ignore,ignore,ignore], so any process-level output it cannot route through appendLog (an uncaught exception stack, a Node warning, node-pty native stderr, an OOM notice) is discarded to the void. A headless remote daemon that dies unexpectedly leaves NO forensic trace beyond appendLog-routed diagnostics, weakening operability of the very process this feature makes long-lived and invisible. REQ-004 permits discard, but redirect-to-log is materially better for a remote daemon.
fix: Open the (bounded) daemon.log fd and pass it as the child stdout/stderr in buildDaemonSpawnSpec so uncaught crashes are captured, rather than discarding to ignore.
resolution: Verified fixed in src/agent/spawn-daemon.ts:35-45: buildDaemonSpawnSpec sets stdio ['ignore', logFd, logFd], routing the detached child stdout/stderr to the daemon log fd instead of discarding to 'ignore'; the bridge opens that fd (bridge.ts:151-175: openSync(logPath,'a') passed into the spec, closed in the parent after spawn). An uncaught daemon crash now leaves a forensic trace. NOTE: separately filed FINDING-021 (LOW) that truncate-at-start can wipe that trace on the next connect.

claim: To drive the idle lifecycle, createDaemonCore runs a DUPLICATE shadow createAgentHandshake in parallel with the one session.ts owns internally, purely to observe establishment, because session.ts exposes no establishment signal even though the store already exposes real bind()/onLeaseRevoked lease events. Two handshake instances must stay in lockstep with no enforcing link (a future change to session.ts handshake acceptance would silently drift the shadow). Worse, the lifecycle interface documents onBind as granted-or-stole-the-lease, but the wiring fires onBind on mere protocol establishment (which precedes and is independent of any F20 lease bind) and onUnbind on connection-end rather than lease-release, conflating protocol-establishment with lease-binding.
fix: Drive the lifecycle from the store bind/unbind (lease) events it already exposes, or add a session.ts onEstablished callback, and delete the shadow handshake; align the onBind/onUnbind naming with the signal actually used.
resolution: Verified: the lifecycle callback vocabulary is renamed to onConnectionEstablished/onConnectionEnded (daemon-lifecycle.ts:41-48), no longer onBind/onUnbind, so it is no longer conflated with the F20 lease bind/steal — the signals are protocol establishment and connection end, wired from the real handshake completion and session shutdown (daemon-server.ts:532-535). The remaining front handshake in createDaemonCore (daemon-server.ts:228-284) is no longer a purely-observational shadow: it is the load-bearing D4' protocol-range acceptance gate (createDaemonAgentHandshake) that REQ-012/REQ-014 deliberately chose to keep session.ts untouched (it rewrites the accepted hello version to the daemon's own so the inner exact-version machine tautologically accepts). Both concrete claims (naming conflation + purely-observational duplicate handshake) no longer hold.

claim: Same-host workspace coexistence REGRESSION: one-daemon-per-agent-dir (D6) + a store-WIDE exclusive F20 lease (session-store.ts 'bound: BoundClient|null') + store.bind() fired on HANDSHAKE (session.ts:228, before establish) means EVERY new socket connection to a host's daemon immediately STEALS the single lease. Two remote workspaces pointed at the same NamedAgent/host (a common setup: e.g. frontend+backend repos on one dev box) now share ONE daemon/store/lease, so opening the second forcibly disconnects the first with a 'lease-stolen' banner (remote-workspace-manager onEvt -> markDisconnected 'lease-stolen'); connections are keyed per-workspace with NO dedup by host, confirming two independent connects. Pre-daemonization each connection ran its OWN agent process, so same-host workspaces coexisted fine — daemonization silently breaks this. The design markets the 'tmux model' (D2/D9) but tmux allows multiple clients attached to DIFFERENT sessions on one server; this whole-store lease permits only ONE attached client per host. No REQ, test, or non-goal addresses concurrent same-host connections — TEST-2433 only asserts 'the bound client is served' (singular), masking the loss of the other.
fix: Make the exclusive lease per-workspace/per-session-group rather than per-host store (bind scoped to the panes a connection attaches), OR route a distinct daemon+socket per workspace instead of per host, so two same-host workspaces coexist as they did pre-daemonization. At minimum add a REQ + test for N concurrent DISTINCT-workspace connections to one host and decide the intended semantics explicitly (the 'lease-stolen' banner is also wrong wording for 'you opened a second workspace to the same box'). propose CONV: a feature that consolidates previously-per-connection server state behind one shared process MUST specify and test how concurrent independent clients coexist (exclusivity granularity / blast radius).
location: src/agent/session.ts:228 (store.bind on handshake) + src/agent/session-store.ts (store-wide lease) + concept D6/D9
resolution: Verified per-workspace daemon in code (not prose): daemon-constants.ts socketFileName(wsToken)=agent-<ws>.sock keys the socket/metadata/log by workspace; ws-token.ts deriveWsToken is deterministic on the workspace id (conforming id as-is, else stable sha256), so distinct workspaces get distinct tokens; services.ts + remote-workspace-manager.ts thread entry.workspaceId (a persisted UUID, reconnect-stable) into BootstrapOptions.daemon.workspaceId. Distinct workspaces therefore resolve distinct sockets => distinct daemons => distinct F20 stores/leases, so a second same-host workspace can no longer steal the first's lease (REQ-018; TEST-2449 two independent cores, TEST-2456 no cross-workspace lease:revoked). The store-wide-lease coexistence regression is eliminated.

claim: Auto-update makes version-drift the COMMON path and creates a host-wide lockout the spec calls impossible. Termhalla auto-updates (updater.ts autoUpdater.quitAndInstall on quit) and remoteAgentInstallPath/handshake use MANIFEST_VERSION (=package.json version, bumps every release). The socket is version-stable while the F15 handshake is exact-version (REQ-012/D4). So the exact motivating flow — close Termhalla, it installs an update, reopen — makes the NEW client hit the OLD still-running daemon -> daemon-version-drift -> fatal, non-destructive. Because the old daemon holds the version-stable agent.sock, a NEW-version daemon can never bind until the old one dies, so EVERY connection to that host (old sessions AND brand-new panes) fails with drift until then. REQ-012's advertised recovery ('the daemon idles out once its sessions end') CANNOT fire for the case the feature exists to serve: a surviving long-lived pane (the running claude from the intake) keeps paneCount>0, and daemon-lifecycle arms only when zero panes AND unbound — so the daemon NEVER idles. Net after an auto-update: the host's entire remote surface is dead until the user SSHes in and manually kills the pid. That is strictly WORSE than pre-daemonization's clean death-on-disconnect (no orphan, no host-wide block), so REQ-012's 'never worse than the pre-daemonization death-on-disconnect' claim is false for this path. The spec never mentions the app's own auto-updater.
fix: Reconcile drift with auto-update (close->update->reopen is the default client lifecycle): negotiate a compatible protocol RANGE for the reattach handshake (concept Q1's rejected option), OR give the drifted-daemon path a sanctioned client-side takeover (graceful drain + respawn) instead of a manual-pid dead-end, OR spawn the new-version daemon on a version-scoped socket so new work is not blocked by a stuck old daemon. At minimum correct the REQ-012 'never worse' wording and ADD a test for the auto-update path with a surviving pane (proving idle-out cannot recover it). propose CONV: any persistence/survival feature MUST analyze its interaction with the app's own auto-updater, since close->update->reopen is the default client lifecycle.
resolution: Verified auto-update reattach in code: shared/remote/daemon-handshake.ts establishes on DAEMON_PROTO_COMPAT (=[WIRE_PROTO], derived) with the app version ADVISORY only. Both legs use the relaxed machine: the daemon core (daemon-server.ts) runs a FRONT createDaemonAgentHandshake observer that accepts on proto-range then rewrites the client hello version to init.version so session.ts's untouched exact-version machine accepts a drifted-but-compatible client; the client (bootstrap-daemon.ts) uses createDaemonClientHandshake. The socket name is version-stable (no app/proto in socketFileName), so a v2 client reattaches to a still-running v1-app-version daemon over the shared socket, sessions survive (TEST-2432 auto-update rig). Only a genuinely proto-breaking release drifts, refused non-destructively (classify.ts daemon-protocol-drift: no kill/removal/upload) and honestly disclosed in REQ-012. The common close->update->reopen lockout is resolved.

claim: The daemon.log 'bounded growth per generation' invariant (REQ-004/CONV-003, truncate-at-start) is illusory for the feature's flagship: a daemon that survives client close/reopen is one-per-host and can live for days/weeks, so a single 'generation' is unbounded in wall-clock time. runDaemon.appendLog writes a line on every recurring diagnostic (socket send failure, chmod failure, core.destroy failure, per-connection 'peer send failed'/'daemon core: peer send failed'); a flapping client/network reconnecting repeatedly grows daemon.log without limit within one generation. It is diagnostics-only (no PTY payload, so not a secrets leak), but the stated per-generation bound does not actually hold for a long-lived daemon.
fix: Cap the log by size (rotate/trim at N KiB) or rate-limit repeated identical diagnostics so the bound holds for a long-lived daemon; or soften the spec's 'bounded growth per generation' claim to acknowledge that a survival daemon's generation is effectively unbounded. Low severity, non-blocking.
resolution: Verified in code: daemon-server.ts createDaemonLogSink (DAEMON_LOG_MAX_BYTES=1MiB, daemon-constants.ts) keeps an in-memory buffer that ring-truncates whole oldest lines on every append until Buffer.byteLength(buf) <= maxBytes, then rewrites the file to that bounded buffer, so a long-lived (wall-clock-unbounded) generation's log can never exceed the cap while the latest diagnostic survives (REQ-004; TEST-2445 drives >cap of diagnostics and asserts the file stays under the cap with the newest line present). The illusory per-generation-only bound is now a real byte bound.

claim: The bridge's stale-remnant reclaim (bridge.ts runBridge, ~lines 146-149) is an UNGUARDED check-then-unlink: it reads daemon.json once (decideDaemonReach -> 'reclaim' on a dead/absent pid) then removeIfExists(socketPath) + removeIfExists(metadataPath) with NO liveness re-check at unlink time. REQ-007's acceptance (CONV-045) requires 'no unguarded check-then-unlink window may exist' and REQ-005 requires reclaim to 'never kill or orphan a LIVE daemon (pid-liveness is checked before any removal)'; the residual race is NOT impossible by the REQ-005 daemon-side guarantee. Two concurrent first-connect bridges (multiple workspaces to one remote, or a reconnect racing a residual connect) can both enter reclaim; if one bridge's initial connect merely TIMES OUT (loaded host, tryConnectOnce 800ms) against a peer daemon that is already bound but has not yet written metadata (metadata is written strictly after listen, REQ-003), its removeIfExists(socketPath) unlinks that LIVE daemon's socket file, orphaning it (it keeps listening on an unlinked inode, permanently unreachable). Worse, that orphaned daemon's later idle cleanup runs safeUnlink(socketPath) on the SAME path -- now owned by a different live daemon -- removing a live daemon's socket+metadata and making its RUNNING sessions unreachable on the next reconnect, defeating the feature's headline survival guarantee (REQ-015).
fix: Delete the bridge-side removeIfExists(socketPath)/removeIfExists(metadataPath) in the reclaim branch and just spawn the daemon. The daemon's own claimSocket() already performs a race-safe reclaim (probe-connectable + pid-liveness gate, unlink, then immediately re-listen in one guarded loop) and truncates its log at start, so the observable (one spawn, reclaim, prior-gen log truncated -- TEST-2425a) is unchanged while the unguarded bridge window disappears. Add a concurrent-reclaim test that forces a bridge unlink to land after a peer daemon has bound but before it writes metadata (the current TEST-2433/2434 rigs never drive that interleaving, so the race is untested).
resolution: Verified fixed in src/agent/bridge.ts: the reclaim/absent branch (runBridge, lines 146-182) now ONLY spawns a fresh daemon and NEVER removes any file — a grep + full read of bridge.ts shows zero unlink/removeIfExists/rm surface (TEST-2457 pins this structurally). All reclaim is delegated to the daemon's serialized claimSocket (daemon-server.ts:391-418: listen -> on-EADDRINUSE probe -> pid-liveness gate via decideDaemonReach -> unlink-of-proven-dead-remnants -> re-bind, inside one guarded loop). The unguarded bridge-side check-then-unlink window that could orphan a live daemon (bound-but-not-yet-metadata) is gone. (The daemon-internal reclaim-vs-concurrent-winner race remains tracked separately under the determinism lens FINDING-006 — not re-filed here.)

claim: connectDaemonAgent parses the bridge status line from stderrAll, which is accumulated as sanitizeStderr(text). sanitizeStderr's CONTROL_CHARS_RE ([\x00-\x1f\x7f-\x9f]+) INCLUDES newline (0x0a) and CR (0x0d), so it collapses every newline to a space. But parseBridgeStatus (bridge-status.ts) finds the 'TERMHALLA_BRIDGE_V1 ' line via stderrText.split('\n') -- on the sanitized text there are no newlines, so the whole stderr is one collapsed line. Whenever the bridge writes ANY stderr BEFORE the status line -- concretely the REQ-011 backend-mismatch diagnostic ('bridge: attaching to a daemon running backend ...'), emitted immediately before the status line -- the collapsed single line no longer startsWith the prefix, so parseBridgeStatus returns null (a trailing non-whitespace diagnostic likewise makes JSON.parse fail on trailing junk). The daemon-flow classifier then emits the REQ-012 drift diagnostic with 'unknown (the bridge status line was unreadable)' even though the daemon's version WAS present on the wire -- violating REQ-012's requirement to name 'the daemon's version (from the bridge status line when readable)'. More broadly it makes REQ-009's 'machine-parseable status line' silently unparseable for any pre-status bridge diagnostic. (Provisioning decisions do not flip, because a fresh spawn with spawned:true has no preceding stderr, so the diagnostic-quality regression is the realized impact.)
fix: Parse the bridge status from a RAW, newline-preserving stderr accumulation rather than the sanitized copy -- e.g. keep stderrRaw += text alongside stderrAll and call parseBridgeStatus(stderrRaw) -- or exclude \n/\r from sanitizeStderr's stripped set so line structure survives (the diag-splitting loop already uses the raw text). Add a bootstrap-daemon vector where the bridge emits a backend-mismatch diagnostic line before the status line and assert the parsed daemon version still reaches the drift diagnostic.
resolution: Verified fixed in src/remote-client/bootstrap-daemon.ts: connectDaemonAgent now keeps a RAW, newline-preserving accumulation 'stderrRaw += text' (line 194) SEPARATE from the sanitized 'stderrTail' (line 195, used only for the diagnostic excerpt), and every bridge-status parse — classifyAndSettle (line 129), the pendingFail defer guard (line 169), and the stderr-arrival settle (line 197) — calls parseBridgeStatus(stderrRaw), NOT the control-char-stripped copy. parseBridgeStatus (bridge-status.ts:52) still splits on newlines, which survive in stderrRaw, so a status line preceded by the REQ-011 backend-mismatch diagnostic parses correctly and the real daemonProto/daemonVersion reach the REQ-012 drift diagnostic. Covered by TEST-2418 (embedded-in-noise) and TEST-2455 (proto-drift with a preceding diagnostic).

claim: decideDaemonReach classifies 'refused + live recorded pid' as 'wait' (never reclaim), and the bridge then poll-connects for DAEMON_SPAWN_WAIT_MS and exits 96 (REQ-007c). Liveness is bare process.kill(pid,0) on the metadata pid. If a daemon CRASHES and the OS later reuses its recorded pid for an unrelated long-lived process, isAlive(pid) stays true, so every reconnect classifies 'wait' -> 96 and NEVER reclaims: the agent dir is permanently wedged -- exactly the 'wedge on a dead socket' failure REQ-007 set out to prevent -- until the reused pid happens to exit. There is no startedAt/age cross-check or bounded-wait-then-guarded-reclaim to break the tie; the only escape is manual kill/clean per the diagnostic. Low probability (pid reuse must hit the exact recorded pid) but unbounded/unrecoverable when it does.
fix: Cross-check liveness beyond bare pid identity before honoring an indefinite 'wait' -- e.g. use daemon.json's startedAt to bound it (a recorded pid whose socket has never accepted across a generous age is treated as stale and reclaimed under the guarded path), or verify the live pid is actually a termhalla daemon. At minimum, record the pid-reuse wedge as a known limitation in REQ-007 so the manual-recovery expectation is explicit.
resolution: Addressed via the disclosure path this LOW finding explicitly offered as an acceptable minimum resolution. REQ-007 (02-spec.md, revision 2) now carries a 'Known v1 limitation (disclosed per FINDING-018)' clause naming the kill(pid,0) pid-reuse wedge and the manual escape hatch, and src/agent/bridge.ts failUnreachable (lines 137-143) emits it at runtime: the 96 diagnostic names the recorded pid, the socket + metadata paths, states NOTHING was removed while the pid is alive, calls out the pid-reuse-by-an-unrelated-process wedge, and gives the manual recovery (terminate the recorded pid, or remove the named files from any SSH shell). The stronger startedAt-age cross-check remains a documented option, not required by the accepted resolution. Covered by TEST-2425(b)/TEST-2435.

claim: remote-workspace-manager.ts's header comment (line 30) says production connects opt in via 'BootstrapOptions.daemon: true', but the actual, correctly-implemented shape (bootstrap.ts, services.ts:109) is 'daemon: { workspaceId: string }' -- a boolean true would not even compile against BootstrapOptions.daemon's type. The doc-comment drifted from the real contract it describes.
fix: Correct the comment to 'BootstrapOptions.daemon: { workspaceId }' (matching docs/features/remote-workspaces.md:69 and CLAUDE.md's own correct wording), so a future reader of this file's header does not copy a shape that never compiled.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-06 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/cleanup, out of scope for this fix pass, tracked for a future hardening feature.

claim: createDaemonLogSink.append rewrites the ENTIRE in-memory buffer (up to DAEMON_LOG_MAX_BYTES = 1 MiB) to disk on EVERY call via writeFileSync(logPath, buf). Once the ring buffer fills, each single diagnostic line forces a full ~1 MiB file rewrite. session.ts emits a diag per skippable-malformed frame (frame skipped, session continues at session.ts:259) WITHOUT ending the connection, and daemon-server emits a diag per failed send, so a peer streaming malformed-but-skippable frames or a flapping/reconnecting client drives per-frame ~1 MiB rewrites — an owner-local write-amplification / disk-thrash vector on the remote host that degrades the very long-lived survival daemon this feature introduces (a thrashing daemon serves its live sessions slowly). The FINDING-010 fix bounded log GROWTH but its full-rewrite-per-line implementation makes each diagnostic cost O(cap) disk IO. TEST-2445 asserts the file-size cap but never the write VOLUME, so the amplification is uncaught.
fix: Append incrementally (appendFileSync or an append-mode fd) and only rotate (rename to a single .prev, start fresh) when the file crosses the cap, instead of a whole-buffer writeFileSync per line — turning O(cap)-per-append into O(line)-per-append with an O(cap) cost only at each rotation. Add a test that bounds total bytes written for N appends. propose CONV: a size-capped diagnostics/ring log for a long-lived process MUST append incrementally and rotate at the cap, never rewrite the whole capped buffer to disk on every line (write amplification scales per-line IO to the cap size).
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-06 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/cleanup, out of scope for this fix pass, tracked for a future hardening feature.

claim: The headless survival daemon is under-instrumented and self-erasing for a process meant to run unattended for days on a remote host. (a) createDaemonLogSink truncates daemon-<ws>.log at each daemon start (REQ-004 MUST), so a crashed daemon crash output (routed to the log per the FINDING-011 fix) is WIPED by the very next connect, which spawns a fresh daemon whose log-sink constructor does writeFileSync(logPath, '') — i.e. the forensic trace is destroyed exactly when an operator reconnects to investigate the lost session. Now that the size cap (FINDING-010) independently bounds growth, truncate-at-start no longer serves growth-bounding; its only realized effect is to defeat FINDING-011 forensic intent in the crash-then-reconnect path (the common case). (b) The daemon writes NO positive lifecycle lines (start with pid/version/proto, socket claim/bind, idle-exit reason, lease-steal, connection establish/end) — only error diagnostics — so on the healthy path daemon-<ws>.log is EMPTY and cannot confirm the daemon ever ran or why it exited, weakening operability of an invisible long-lived process.
fix: Preserve the prior generation on start (rename daemon-<ws>.log to daemon-<ws>.log.prev before truncating) so a crash trace survives the reconnect that spawns the successor; and emit positive lifecycle diagnostics (start pid/version/proto, claim/bind, idle-exit reason, lease-steal, connection-count transitions) so the log is a usable operability record on the healthy path too. propose CONV: a headless long-lived daemon/service MUST preserve the previous generation diagnostics across a restart (never truncate the only crash trace in place) and MUST log its own lifecycle transitions (start/ready/exit-reason), not only error diagnostics.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-06 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/cleanup, out of scope for this fix pass, tracked for a future hardening feature.

claim: REQ-003 MUST (a socket path exceeding the AF_UNIX limit measured in BYTES MUST fail daemon start with a specific error naming the path and the limit, never a raw EINVAL) is enforced ONLY in bootstrapDaemonEndpoint (daemon-server.ts:74-101), which is TEST-ONLY: production never calls it. main.ts calls runDaemon, whose real bind path runDaemon then claimSocket then listenOnce (daemon-server.ts:341-418) calls server.listen(socketPath) with NO length guard, so an over-long POSIX socket path throws a raw platform error (EINVAL/ENAMETOOLONG) that the claimSocket catch swallows, treats as a bind failure, then loops the probe/reclaim path up to maxAttempts=200 (tens of seconds) before returning null and exiting the daemon 0 SILENTLY; the bridge then reports a generic exit-96 timeout, never the REQ-003 named error. Reachable in production: the default socket is agentDir/agent-<wsToken>.sock and deriveWsToken maps every non-conforming (or ~50-plus-char) workspace id to a 64-char token (ws- plus 61 hex), so with the default ~/.termhalla/agent dir and an 8-plus-char remote username the encoded path exceeds 107 bytes; a non-ASCII home dir hits it at fewer characters (encoding/locale-dependent, the same byte-vs-char class as FINDING-007). No test exercises the over-long REAL path (TEST-2412 covers only the seam; TEST-2454 uses a short ws-derived path), so the byte-correct guard is production-dead.
fix: Compose the byte-length guard into the production bind path: have claimSocket/listenOnce run the same Buffer.byteLength(socketPath, utf8) greater-than AF_UNIX_PATH_MAX check and fail fast with the named path-and-limit error BEFORE the 200-attempt claim loop, ideally by routing the real bind through bootstrapDaemonEndpoint or extracting the guard into a shared helper both call, so the tested seam and production cannot diverge. Add a POSIX-guarded real-socket over-length vector (a greater-than-107-byte ws-derived path) asserting the daemon fails fast with the named error, never a swallowed EINVAL or a silent exit-0 plus generic bridge 96. propose CONV: a validation guard whose contract is a MUST on a real operation (bind/write/spawn) MUST live on the production code path, not only in a pure seam the tests exercise; a seam-only guard production never calls is green in CI and dead in prod.
resolution: Verified fixed by code read. The byte-accurate over-long-path guard is now composed into the PRODUCTION bind path: runDaemon (daemon-server.ts:448-449) calls the shared checkSocketPathLength(socketPath, process.platform) and throws synchronously BEFORE claimSocket's 200-attempt claim/reclaim loop ever starts. checkSocketPathLength (daemon-guard.ts:104-117) is the ONE shared implementation — Buffer.byteLength(socketPath,'utf8') vs AF_UNIX_PATH_MAX=107, win32-exempt — and BOTH the pure seam bootstrapDaemonEndpoint (daemon-server.ts:79) and production runDaemon call it, so they can never diverge. The synchronous throw inside the Promise executor rejects runDaemon()'s promise; main.ts:76-92 awaits it in try/catch and names it on stderr ('daemon failed: <path-and-limit message>'), and (when detached) the same error also reaches the ws-keyed daemon log via the spawn spec's log-fd stdio (FINDING-011). TEST-2459 (agent-daemon-process.test.ts:586, describe.skipIf win32) spawns a real --daemon with a 160-char over-long --socket and asserts it exits nonzero FAST (well under DAEMON_SPAWN_WAIT_MS, never 'looping'), never exit 0, stderr contains the offending path + names the byte limit, and never leaks a raw EINVAL/ENAMETOOLONG. The 'seam-only guard, production-dead' contract violation no longer holds. (Skip-gated on this Windows dev box, but the production code path and test logic are correct on inspection.)

claim: The headline 'survival across client close/reopen' holds for only TWO of the THREE going-away gestures, and the implementation silently KILLS the remote session on the third. Verified in code: (1) QUIT APP -> RemoteWorkspaceManager.stop() -> teardownWire drops the ssh wire only, NO pty:kill, so the detached daemon + PTYs survive and reattach on reopen (the specified scenario, TEST-2429). (2) banner DISCONNECT -> disconnectWorkspace() -> teardownWire, survives. (3) CLOSE the remote WORKSPACE TAB -> store.ts closeWorkspace -> teardownPanes(paneIds) -> api.ptyKill per pane -> RemoteWorkspaceManager.kill(paneId), which when connected sends CH.ptyKill to the daemon, so the daemon KILLS the remote PTYs and the running claude dies. So the single most natural per-session gesture a tmux-parity user would make -- close the workspace window expecting to reattach later -- destroys exactly the session the daemon exists to preserve: the precise loss ('reopened but claude was gone') the feature was built to prevent. This asymmetry (quit=survive, disconnect=survive, close-tab=kill) is undocumented, unspecified, and untested: concept D8 assumed 'daemonization lands with no client change' and never re-examined the pre-existing closeWorkspace kill path; REQ-015 exercises only the app-close/teardownWire shape via the fake lane; no REQ/test covers closing a remote workspace tab. It also contradicts the concept's marketed 'true tmux-parity' -- in tmux, closing the client window DETACHES and the session persists.
fix: Decide and specify per-gesture survival semantics for remote workspaces. Either make closing a remote workspace tab DETACH (route through teardownWire, leaving the daemon+PTYs to survive and idle-reap) rather than pty:kill its remote panes, matching the quit/disconnect paths; OR keep the kill but add an explicit, distinct 'keep remote session running' affordance and document that a plain close discards. Add a REQ enumerating each teardown gesture and an integration test that closes a remote workspace tab then reconnects the same workspaceId and asserts the pane is reattachable (survives) unless the user explicitly killed. propose CONV: a survival/persistence feature MUST enumerate EVERY client teardown gesture (quit, disconnect, close-tab, delete/re-home workspace, window-close/undock) and specify + test for each whether it detaches-and-survives or kills the underlying session -- an unspecified gesture that kills silently defeats the feature.
location: src/renderer/store.ts:455 closeWorkspace/teardownPanes vs stop/disconnectWorkspace teardownWire; REQ-015 / concept D8
resolution: Verified fixed by direct code read of the round-3 REQ-019 routing. store.ts closeWorkspace (lines 455-485) now branches through the pure api-free core routeWorkspaceTeardown (src/renderer/store/pane-ops.ts:109-118): for a REMOTE workspace (isRemote: !!ws.home) it calls ONLY detach() -> disconnectRemote(id,{forget:true}) + pruneRemoteStates, issuing ZERO teardownPanes/ptyKill; for a LOCAL workspace it calls kill(paneId)=>teardownPanes([paneId]) per pane, byte-identical to before. The detach lands via remote-slice disconnectRemote -> the additive {forget} on the existing remote:disconnect IPC (register-remote validates the shape) -> RemoteWorkspaceManager.disconnectWorkspace(id,{forget:true}) (line 171-185), which runs teardownWire (line 597-616 kills ONLY entry.wire, the local ssh child, never sends CH.ptyKill) then deletes the entry (no ghost in currentStates). So closing a remote workspace tab now detaches; the daemon and its PTYs survive for the F18 reattach -- the original silent-kill defect is gone. Confirmed the individual-pane close (closePane) still keeps teardownPanes([paneId]) (corollary b). Note: the round-3 fix also introduced two new gaps I filed separately (the live-session close-tab never idle-reaps + false doc claim; the now-inaccurate close confirm dialog).

claim: src/shared/remote/daemon-handshake.ts (168 lines) is a near-total copy-paste of src/shared/remote/handshake.ts (181 lines): describeNonFrame, alreadyDone, the evaluateHello/evaluateDaemonHello frame-shape+role validation body (frame-type check, parseWireMessage call, role check), and the createAgentHandshake/createDaemonAgentHandshake + createClientHandshake/createDaemonClientHandshake factory shapes (helloFrame(), single-use onMessage guard) are all duplicated verbatim, with only the proto/version acceptance predicate actually differing (exact WIRE_PROTO+exact version vs DAEMON_PROTO_COMPAT range+advisory version). A bugfix to the shared hello-parsing/role-validation logic (e.g. a parseWireMessage edge case, the single-use guard, the non-frame description helper) has to be remembered and applied in both files.
fix: Factor the common hello-validation core (frame-shape/role check + parseWireMessage + the single-use onMessage/alreadyDone/describeNonFrame plumbing) into one parameterized helper in handshake.ts, taking an injected 'is this proto/version acceptable' predicate; have both the exact-version machines and the daemon-flow relaxed machines call it, varying only the predicate and the resulting ProtocolFailure reason/message.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/quality, tracked for a hardening feature.

claim: runDaemon (src/agent/daemon-server.ts, ~lines 434-440) and runBridge (src/agent/bridge.ts, ~lines 113-118) each independently re-derive the SAME six-line endpoint-path sequence: artifactPath = process.argv[1] ?? process.cwd(); agentDir = dirname(artifactPath); ws = <wsToken> ?? 'default'; socketPath = <override> ?? join(agentDir, socketFileName(ws)); metadataPath = join(agentDir, metadataFileName(ws)); logPath = join(agentDir, logFileName(ws)). daemon-guard.ts is already the designated shared home for daemon/bridge cross-cutting logic (per its own header) but does not host this. The duplication has already begun to drift stylistically: daemon-server.ts wraps the ws-default in a named resolveScope() helper while bridge.ts inlines the equivalent 'args.wsToken ?? default' expression, so the 'default' literal is a magic string repeated in two independent places with no shared constant.
fix: Extract one shared resolveDaemonEndpointPaths(artifactPath, { wsToken, socketPath }) helper into daemon-guard.ts (or a new daemon-paths.ts) returning { agentDir, socketPath, metadataPath, logPath }, used by both runDaemon and runBridge; give the 'default' scope literal one named constant.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/quality, tracked for a hardening feature.

claim: The daemon's diagnostics log file (daemon-<wsToken>.log) is created/rewritten via bare writeFileSync(logPath, ...) in createDaemonLogSink (daemon-server.ts lines ~111 and ~129) with NO explicit mode, and bridge.ts's fallback openSync(logPath, 'a') (~line 153) likewise passes no mode -- both default to Node's 0o666-minus-ambient-umask. Unlike the socket (created 0600 via a temporarily restrictive process.umask(0o077) around listen(), FINDING-005) and the metadata file (explicitly writeFileSync(metadataPath, ..., { mode: 0o600 })), the log file inherits whatever the process's ambient umask happens to be at first creation -- in runDaemon the log sink is constructed strictly AFTER claimSocket resolves and the restrictive umask has already been restored, and in the bridge-spawn fallback path the file is opened under the ssh exec channel's own default umask. Under a common umask of 022 the file lands at mode 0644 -- world-READABLE on the exact same shared multi-tenant remote host that FINDING-005 already identified as this feature's threat model -- exposing diagnostic content (workspace tokens, pids, install paths, home-directory structure, and every diag()-routed error-branch message from session.ts/session-store.ts/daemon-server.ts) to any other local user on the box. A more permissive umask (e.g. 000) would make it world-WRITABLE, letting another local user inject arbitrary bytes (including terminal escape sequences) into a file the bridge's own manual-recovery diagnostic tells a human to 'cat' from an SSH shell.
fix: Give the log file the same owner-only treatment as its socket/metadata siblings: open/create it with an explicit restrictive mode (e.g. writeFileSync(logPath, '', { mode: 0o600 }) on first creation in createDaemonLogSink, and pass 0o600 to bridge.ts's fallback openSync(logPath, 'a', 0o600)), or wrap the initial create in the same temporarily-restrictive-umask discipline the socket bind already uses. Add a POSIX integration test (mirroring TEST-2454) asserting the real log file's mode is 0600 at first observable existence.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/quality, tracked for a hardening feature.

claim: The round-3 REQ-019 fix (close-tab DETACHES-and-FORGETS) creates an unbounded-orphan case the spec's own consequence-stating discipline never discloses, for exactly the flagship survival scenario. daemon-lifecycle maybeArm() arms the idle timer only when establishedCount===0 AND paneCount===0, so a daemon with a still-LIVE pane (the running claude the feature exists to preserve) NEVER idle-reaps. closeWorkspace's remote branch also forgets the manager entry (disconnectRemote {forget:true} -> entry deleted from currentStates), so after a close-tab the workspace vanishes from remote:current with no in-app visibility of, or affordance to reap, the still-running remote process. Yet REQ-019's corollary and docs/features/remote-workspaces.md:104-105 assert 'an unreopened workspace's daemon idle-reaps on its own once its established-connection count reaches zero (REQ-006) - idle timeouts bound the accumulation, not a kill on close.' That claim omits REQ-006's paneCount===0 gate and is FALSE for the survival case: close-and-never-reopen with a running process leaks an orphaned daemon+session indefinitely (until host reboot), unbounded by any idle timeout - the very orphan-accumulation D3 claims to bound. The gap is untested: TEST-2464 pty:kills the pane BEFORE the tab close, so it only exercises the empty-daemon reap, never the live-pane orphan. This is the CONV-054 consequence-stating discipline the spec applied to version-drift (REQ-012) missing here.
fix: Correct REQ-019/REQ-017 and remote-workspaces.md to state HONESTLY (CONV-054 discipline, as REQ-012 did for drift) that a close-tab-detach of a STILL-RUNNING remote session is NOT idle-reaped: the daemon+session persist until the session's own process exits or the user manually terminates it (SSH, or reopen + per-pane kill) - idle timeouts bound accumulation ONLY for sessions that have already ended. Add a test that closes the tab while a pane is LIVE and asserts the intended semantics (daemon pid survives, no reap) rather than only the pre-killed/empty reap. Optionally add a reap-on-close affordance (already noted as out-of-scope, but then the leak must be disclosed, not hidden behind a false idle-reap claim). propose CONV: a survival/persistence feature that DETACHES-and-FORGETS on a teardown gesture MUST test that gesture with a LIVE underlying session (not only a pre-killed/empty one) and MUST disclose whether that live session is ever automatically reaped; an idle-reap gated on the session already being gone does not bound accumulation for the surviving-session case the feature exists to create.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/quality, tracked for a hardening feature.

claim: The close-workspace confirmation dialog now lies for remote workspaces. Both the tab context-menu (WorkspaceTabs.tsx:184) and the keyboard shortcut (App.tsx:154-156) prompt 'Close workspace "name"? Its terminals will be closed.' before calling closeWorkspace. After the round-3 REQ-019 fix, closing a REMOTE workspace DETACHES - its terminals are NOT closed, they keep running on the daemon for reattach. So the user is explicitly warned their terminals will be terminated, clicks OK, and they silently survive instead (and, per the companion finding, may leak with no reap). Pre-REQ-019 the warning was accurate (close-tab killed the panes); the fix changed the behavior but left the adjacent warning copy contradicting it. A user who WANTS to end the remote work reads this confirm as doing so and is misled.
fix: Make the confirm copy conditional on ws.home: for a remote workspace state that the terminals will keep running on the remote and can be reattached by reopening the workspace (they are detached, not closed), so the warning matches the REQ-019 detach behavior; keep the existing 'terminals will be closed' text for local workspaces. Apply in both WorkspaceTabs.tsx and App.tsx (the two confirm sites).
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/quality, tracked for a hardening feature.

claim: The REQ-019 close-tab detach leaks the manager paneIndex. disconnectWorkspace(id, { forget: true }) with tracked panes deletes the entry from this.entries but NEVER prunes this.paneIndex (the paneId -> workspaceId index). paneIndex is cleared ONLY in kill() (line 267) and prunePane() (line 594): kill() is deliberately skipped on the detach path (that is the whole point of detach), and prunePane() is reachable only through the now-deleted entry own readopt/exit callbacks, so neither runs. Every close-tab detach therefore leaks one paneIndex entry per tracked pane in a process-lifetime main-process singleton, unbounded over a long session across many distinct remote workspaces closed and never reopened. Latent smell: owns()/isAdoptable() return true for these dead pane ids and register-pty consults owns() to route pty ops (harmless in practice only because pane ids are UUIDs that never collide). Pre-0024 this same gesture routed through kill() and DID prune paneIndex; the FINDING-023 detach routing removed the only cleanup path without replacing it. TASK-022 acceptance asserts the entry is gone from currentStates() but never asserts paneIndex is pruned, so the leak is untested.
fix: In the disconnectWorkspace forget branch, before this.entries.delete(workspaceId), prune paneIndex for every pane the entry still tracks (iterate entry.panes.keys() -> this.paneIndex.delete(paneId)) WITHOUT killing the remote PTY. Add a manager-routing assertion that after a forget disconnect with tracked panes, owns() returns false for those pane ids.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/quality, tracked for a hardening feature.

claim: REQ-019 states an unreopened workspace daemon is idle-reaped once its established-connection count reaches 0, but daemon-lifecycle maybeArm arms the idle timer only when establishedCount===0 AND paneCount===0. A close-tab detach ends the connection (establishedCount -> 0) yet leaves the panes running on the daemon (paneCount > 0), which is precisely the survival the feature exists to provide, so the daemon does NOT idle-reap for the flagship case (a detached workspace with a running claude or an interactive shell). The spec reap claim therefore overstates automatic reclamation: for the common detach-with-live-panes case the daemon persists until the pane process exits or a manual reap. Compounding this, there is no app-side affordance to enumerate or reap detached remote daemons/sessions per host, no server-side max-detached-age or max-session cap, and no user feedback on close that a remote session was left running. Over time a user accumulates invisible, uncapped, long-lived remote daemons + PTYs across closed-but-never-reopened workspaces, reclaimable only by the pane own exit or a manual SSH kill, a material operability/scalability change from pre-0024 clean death-on-disconnect for this default gesture. Distinct from the deferred FINDING-021 (log diagnostics): this is fleet visibility + reap + an inaccurate spec reap claim, not diagnostics.
fix: Correct the REQ-019 reap wording to state reap requires zero established connections AND zero live panes (so a detach with running panes persists by design), disclose in remote-workspaces.md per CONV-054 that a close-tab detach leaves the remote daemon + PTYs running until reopened, the pane exits, or a manual reap, and track a follow-up for app-side detached-session enumeration/reap and/or a server-side max-detached-age cap so the fleet stays bounded and operable. propose CONV: a survival/persistence feature that leaves server-side processes running after the client default going-away gesture MUST provide, or explicitly defer with disclosure, a way to ENUMERATE and REAP those processes from the client - a persistent process the user cannot see or stop from the app is an operability orphan.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/quality, tracked for a hardening feature.

claim: The long-lived daemon's net.Server has NO 'error' handler after listen() succeeds, and main.ts installs no process-level uncaughtException handler. listenOnce attaches server.once('error', onError) only for the bind, then server.removeListener('error', onError) in the listen callback (line 347); runDaemon thereafter only wires server.on('connection') (line 541) and per-socket sock.on('error') (line 562) — the SERVER object itself is left with no 'error' listener for its entire serving lifetime. A post-listen server 'error' event (Node emits one on an accept-time failure such as EMFILE/ENFILE under file-descriptor exhaustion — plausible for a survival daemon that runs for days accumulating connections/panes/FDs, or other libuv accept errors) is therefore an unhandled EventEmitter 'error' → thrown → uncaught exception → the whole daemon process dies. That kills EVERY surviving PTY for that workspace (a running claude included), which is precisely the catastrophe daemonization exists to prevent. It also introduces a third daemon-exit path that REQ-002's invariant forbids ('the daemon exits ONLY via REQ-006 idle/signal — never because a connection ended'): an accept-error crash is neither idle nor signal.
fix: Re-attach a durable, process-surviving server-level error handler immediately after claimSocket returns the bound server in runDaemon, e.g. server.on('error', (e) => appendLog('daemon server error (accept/listener): ' + String(e))) so an accept-time error is logged and the daemon keeps serving its existing panes/connections instead of crashing. Optionally add a top-level process.on('uncaughtException'/'unhandledRejection') backstop in main.ts's daemon path that logs to the daemon log and keeps the process alive (panes survive) rather than tearing down. Cover with a unit that emits a synthetic 'error' on the server after listen and asserts the process/store survives. propose CONV: any long-lived net.Server/listener (a process meant to outlive individual connections) MUST retain a process-surviving 'error' handler for the whole serving lifetime — never leave the listener with its bind-time error handler removed and none re-attached, so an accept-time error (EMFILE/etc.) cannot crash the process.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0024-agent-daemonization-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness/quality, tracked for a hardening feature.
